title: Chapter 11: Armor and Will
paragraphs: Morning in the Mechanics' Guild began not with the singing of elven birds, but with a sound painfully like the roar of a jackhammer. Zhenya, who hadn't closed his eyes all night, tiredly wiped the grease from his face with a dirty rag and with an open, almost fatherly pride, stroked his first full-fledged creation. On the heavy oak workbench lay a massive steel gauntlet reaching to the very forearm. It more resembled a piece of heavy armored military-era exoskelton than a magical artifact. Reinforced polyurethane tubes stretched along the rough but reliable metal plates, and an alarmingly pulsating, threatening shard of a red demonic crystal was embedded in the wrist area.
"Hey, jock," Zhenya rasped, giving engineer Brann a grateful look. "Try on your new arm."

Ravan, who had slept directly on the cold floor by the barrel, slowly rose. He looked at the mechanism with obvious disbelief, but obediently slid inside the once-broken, then elven-healed vulnerable flesh. The gauntlet hissed maliciously, clamping tightly with pneumatic fasteners. Ravan reflexively wiggled his fingers—the mechanism responded immediately with a pleasant, heavy metallic clank. The weight of the construction was huge, pulling to the ground, but for the former dentist-bodybuilder it seemed a perfect balance.
"Strike that defective anvil," Zhenya grinned, stepping back a couple of paces.

Ravan swung heavily. At the moment his fist went forward, the pneumatic piston fired, many times, many times amplifying the natural kinetics of the muscles. The steel fist hit the monolithic anvil with a frightening speed. A deafening explosion of metal echoed. The anvil, weighing half a ton, didn't just move—it split in two, raising a whole cloud of cast-iron dust.

Ravan froze, staring at his armored hand. His chest rose heavily, greedily. For the first time since falling into this hell, he felt absolute control over the situation. Meat could fail, break under the blow of ancient magic. But cold-blooded steel doesn't feel pain. A dangerous, predatory fire flared in his eyes: with this thing he would get home at any cost and tear apart anyone who stood in the way to his son.

"And for you, commander, here it is," Zhenya threw a strange-looking crossbow to Alexander. It didn't have an archaic string, but it had a modern drum-type magazine and a complex magnetic bolt acceleration system, powered by a small energy core taken from a golem. "Our earthly ergonomics. Light, fast-firing. Lethal range—one hundred meters, bolts pierce chitin like butter."

Alexander deftly threw the weapon to his shoulder, checking the balance. The weapon sat in his hands as if molded. The Isekai team was forever ceasing to be a bunch of frightened people in rags. They were growing armor, fangs, and a steel will.

The workshop doors burst open with a crash. Avotu stood on the threshold. His armor was generously covered with fresh gray ash, and the black flame barely smoldered on his head, threatening to go out. The great hero looked as if crushed by the gravity of despair.

"My brother is dead. Like everyone else," he said hollowly, in a colorless voice, walking heavily inside. He wasn't looking at them; he was looking into the void. "I've been chasing shadows for two years. Ripples... portals are the blind, rotting breath of reality itself. The void has no master. And we cannot close it."

The silence hanging in the workshop was heavier than lead. The darkness of the past covered Avotu over the head. The illusion that he was saving the world, for the sake of which he had closed his eyes to Biotrix's death, crumbled to dust. The realization that this was the final, terminal reality pressed on his psyche with the force of the ocean.
"So... we just live here," Anar finally stretched lazily, indifferently spinning a blue spark in his fingers. "Until we're devoured. Well, honestly. At least no taxes."

But they were not destined to wallow in psychological paralysis. An icky, long siren wailed outside the windows. The howl of the alarm horn of Agdash, thick and panicky, cut through the morning fog. The ground beneath their feet trembled slightly, unpleasantly.
"Breakthrough! A breakthrough directly inside the city perimeter!" an elven boy screamed, running past the workshop with a twisted face. "The Eastern Mine! A portal opened right in the pit!"
Blood immediately drained from Alexander's face. The Eastern Mine.
Where a second ago Avotu was philosophizing about hopelessness, for the earthly people the world narrowed to one point. Yura was there.
"Let's go!" Alexander clicked the bolt of his mag-crossbow with a threatening metallic clank.

There were no arguments. There were no philosophical discussions about the meaning of existence. Ravan clanked his pneumatic fist, Zhenya grabbed a piece of reforged rebar, and Anar immediately erased the sleepy expression from his face, allowing dangerous blue discharges to run down his spine. They rushed into the thick of the raging hell not for the salvation of an abstract dying world. They ran to save their brother.

Avotu, stunned by their animal, uncompromising loyalty, froze for a moment. And then the elf's hand rested on the hilt of the Nature's Blade, and the flame flared up with a new, cleansing power. He stepped after them.
